cards.front: What is the causal LTI convolution formula used in the BIBO proof?
What is the absolute integrability condition on h(t)?
If the input is bounded by M, what bound do we get for the output?
Why are absolute values used in the integral test?
cards.back: They prevent positive and negative parts from cancelling and hiding the true magnitude of the impulse response.
Yes. Its absolute integral is 1, so it satisfies the BIBO condition.
No. The integral of 1 from 0 to infinity diverges, so the condition fails.

cards.front: What kind of stability does absolute integrability guarantee?
cards.back: It guarantees BIBO stability: every bounded input produces a bounded output.

cards.front: How is BIBO stability related to the earlier pole discussion?
cards.back: Pole locations shape h(t), and the shape of h(t) determines whether |h(t)| is integrable. So BIBO stability is the input-output face of the same dynamics.

cards.front: How can you reconstruct the BIBO proof from scratch?
cards.back: Write convolution, take absolute values, bound the shifted input by M, factor M out, and ask whether the remaining integral of |h| is finite.
